segments.s186-g17: کرا is an archaic interrogative 'who'. باشد here is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of بودن used to express possibility or speculation in a rhetorical question ('could be' / 'would be'). اندر میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'in/among the midst of the host' (اندر + میان = 'in the middle of'); سپاه is the noun 'host/army'. Note the verb (باشد) appears before the locative phrase and subject (a typical flexible word order in classical Persian).
segments.s186-g18: چنین modifies the following nominal phrase meaning 'such' / 'so'. آلت is 'instrument/implement' and ساز here functions as a noun meaning 'device/means' (not a verb), so آلتِ ساز = 'implements / instruments'; و links the two coordinated nouns; این points to the following noun 'this', and دستگاه is 'apparatus / array / system'. Together this is a noun phrase describing the kind of equipment being questioned.
segments.s186-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. گفتار او را is the object phrase 'his speech/words' with را marking it as definite/object; note the subject (تخوار) follows the verb here, a permitted and emphatic word order in classical Persian. تخوار functions here as the person who hears (vocative/name or substantive 'the wretch').
segments.s186-g20: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed expression 'gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus / such', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces reported/direct speech here ('that / saying'), and ای شهریار is a vocative 'O king' addressing the ruler; in poetry the particle که commonly leads into the words actually spoken.
segments.s186-g21: پس here indicates sequence/position ('then' / 'after'), and پشت means 'back/behind' so پسِ پشت او = 'immediately behind him'. طوس is the proper name Tūs and سپهبد is his title 'spahbed' (general). بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order places the locative phrase before the nominative/title for poetic emphasis.
segments.s186-g22: که begins a relative clause describing the spahbed ('who...'). در + کینه is a locative/instrumental phrase 'in/with hatred' (i.e. 'harboring a grudge'), پیکار is 'battle/struggle', او is the pronoun 'him' (object of the hostility), بد is a predicative adjective meaning 'hostile/unfavourable', and بود is the copula 'was'. The literal order 'در کینه پیکار او بد بود' = 'was bad/hostile in hatred toward him' → 'harbored a grudge and was hostile toward him in battle.'
segments.s186-g23: درفشی is 'standard-bearer' or 'banner-man' (subject). پس پشت او is again the locative 'behind him'. دیگرست is the copular predicate 'is different / another' (here 'there was another'); the clause states that a different standard-bearer was positioned behind him.
segments.s186-g24: چو is the comparative/simile 'like / as'. خورشید تابان is 'the shining sun' (خورشید = sun; تابان = shining/illustrious). بدو is a dative/locative pronoun 'to him/unto it' referring back to the standard-bearer or banner, and پیکرست is a contracted poetic form of 'پیکر است' meaning 'is a form / has the form'. Together: 'his form was like the shining sun.'
segments.s186-g1: سواران is a plural subject ('the horsemen/riders') placed before the past tense verb رسیدند (3rd person plural past). رسیدند is simple past 'they arrived/reached'. بر introduces a location ('on/upon') and تیغ کوه is a genitive/nominal phrase 'the point/ridge of the mountain' (تیغ here = 'point, ridge' + کوه 'mountain'). The whole phrase means 'the horsemen reached the mountain ridge.'
segments.s186-g2: سپاه is the subject 'the host/army'. اندر آمد is a classical compound where اندر ('in/within') combines with آمد ('came') to mean 'entered' or 'came in'. گروها گروه is a poetic, emphatic juxtaposition ('groups, group') — repetition/parallel nouns to mean 'group after group' or 'band after band'. Word order places the adverbial/locational اندر before the verb, as is typical in older Persian.
segments.s186-g3: سپردار is the head noun 'the commander'. با 'with' marks accompaniment. نیزه‌ور here names the fighting type 'lancers' or 'spear‑men'. The numeral phrase 'سی هزار' follows in the line (poetic inversion is common), giving 'with thirty thousand lancers'. The noun stays bare and the whole chunk functions as an apposition describing the commander.
segments.s186-g4: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/everyone' placed before the noun رزمجوی ('combatant, warrior'). از در کارزار is a classical locative phrase: از + در + کارزار yields the sense 'in/within the battle' (از در used idiomatically for being 'in the midst of' something). Together: 'all combatants in the field of battle.'
segments.s186-g5: سوار و پیاده lists the two classes 'horse (riders/cavalry) and foot (infantry)'. بزرین is an adjective 'great' modifying کمر; poetic compounds like 'بزرین کمر' give a collective sense — here best rendered 'great in strength' or 'mighty‑backed' (کمر can figuratively mean 'strength, backbone'). Word order places the two nouns in coordination, then the descriptive phrase.
segments.s186-g6: همه repeated before each predicate is a distributive/emphatic 'all' — 'all sword‑bearers and all lancers.' تیغ‌دار is a compound adjective/noun 'sword‑bearing' and نیزه‌ور repeats the earlier term 'lancer/spear‑bearer'. The coordinating و simply links the two halves.
segments.s186-g7: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. بس here means 'abundance' or 'so many' (zabus = 'through so many/owing to abundance'), so ز بس introduces a causal/illustrative list. ترگ زرین is a noun + adjective 'golden shred(s)/shreds of cloth' (ترگ = 'rag, shred'), and زرین درفش is 'golden standard/banner' — two coordinated items joined by و.
segments.s186-g8: Again ز = 'from/of'. گوپال is an archaic/poetic word here; its senses listed include 'cowherd' or a proper name (Gopala). In this context it appears as a substantive combined with زرین ('golden'), likely a poetic name for a kind of ornament, banner, or cuirass — the line uses terse compounds and names rather than explicit description. زرینه کفش is 'gilded shoes' (زرینه = 'gilded/splendid' modifying کفش 'shoes'); adjective–noun order is compressed for verse. The whole segment continues the catalogue of golden items.
segments.s186-g57: This is a nominal clause where درفشی (the banner) is the subject followed by the interrogative کجا (literally “where/how” used rhetorically for “what/with what”), then the predicate غرم دارد نشان: دارد is the present-tense third-person verb ‘has/possesses’, showing possession of نشان (a sign/token). The word order is subject — interrogative modifier — predicate, typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s186-g58: ز introduces the source or provenance of the token (here, ‘from’ or ‘belonging to’), followed by two proper names (بهرام گودرز) and an epithet/or descriptive compound (کشوادگان) that modifies or identifies Goudarz. The whole segment functions as a prepositional phrase specifying whose token/sign it is.
segments.s186-g59: همه is the universal subject ‘all/everyone’, followed by شیرمردند, an archaic/poetic plural predicate meaning ‘they are brave men’ (literally ‘lion‑men’). The coordinating phrase و گرد و سوار lists the kinds of men (‘troop and horseman’ or ‘foot and horse’), with و linking the items; the phrase as a whole describes the composition of that ‘all’.
segments.s186-g60: یکایک is an adverb ‘one by one’ modifying the following verb phrase; بگویم here is the poetically inflected verb form meaning ‘I shall tell / I will recount’ (first person), and درازست کار is a verbless clause in which درازست (‘is long’) predicatively qualifies کار (‘the affair / the matter’). The sequence therefore says the speaker will relate things individually, but the matter is lengthy.
segments.s186-g61: چو here functions temporally as ‘when’; یک‌یک ‘one by one’ marks successive action, and بگفت is the simple past/poetic past ‘he said / he spoke’. از نشان identifies the topic of their speech (‘about/of the token’), and گوان (‘like/thus’) is a poetic particle meaning ‘such’ or ‘of that sort’, qualifying نشان. The clause therefore sets up the time when each person described his token.
segments.s186-g62: بپیش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning ‘before / forward into the presence of’; فرود here is a verbal or verbal‑noun sense ‘alighted/stepped (down/forward)’ used poetically to indicate movement toward the king, and آن شه خسروان is the title phrase ‘that king/ruler (king of kings)’. Together the segment describes the king coming forward (into view or presence) upon the reports.
segments.s186-g63: مهان و کهان را همه is a topicalized/constrasting list: مهان (‘the great/guests/nobles’) and کهان (‘the world/people’) with را marking the object/topic and همه emphasizing ‘all’. بنگرید is a third‑person plural present/subjunctive form meaning ‘they looked’ or an exhortative/optative ‘let all look’, so the segment says that everyone — high and low — gazed.
segments.s186-g64: ز شادی is an ablative/causal phrase ‘from/because of joy’, رخش is the possessor/subject ‘his steed’, and همچو گل is a simile ‘like a flower’. بشکفید is a poetic present/plural form ‘bloomed / burst into bloom’. The clause thus uses a causal phrase plus simile to describe the steed’s joyful display as if it ‘bloomed like a flower’.
segments.s186-g25: برادر پدر تست is a compact possessive chain: برادرِ پدرِ تَست — "the brother of your father" (poetic omission of the ezafe is common). با + [فر و کام] means "with farr and kām" (with royal divine glory and with desire/fulfillment); this whole chunk is a noun phrase describing the person. Note تست here is not the modern verb "to test" but an archaic/poetic possessive/copula form (meaning roughly "is yours" or part of the possessive chain), which is why the literal senses given in the word list do not match this usage.
segments.s186-g26: This is an appositive/title phrase: سپهبد (the military title) followed by the personal name فریبرز and then کاوس نام, where نام at the end functions poetically to mean "named/called". The word order is title + name + 'نام' ("named"), a common compact construction in epic Persian.
segments.s186-g27: پسش (= پشتِ او / "behind him/after him") begins the clause. ماه پیکر is a compound adjective phrase literally "moon‑formed/ moon‑faced figure" (ماه used metaphorically for beauty/roundness) modifying درفشی بزرگ, where درفشی بزرگ means "a large / great banner/standard"; poetic style omits the connective ezafe and places the descriptive elements compactly.
segments.s186-g28: دلیران بسیار is a nominal phrase meaning "many brave men" (دلیران = "the brave", بسیار = "many/very numerous" in context). و connects the next image; گردی سترگ is a compact poetic image literally "a (great) dust/cloud" (گردی) + سترگ, here producing the sense of a large, imposing cloud (of dust kicked up by the host) — word senses given in the list are partially mismatched to the epic usage, so سترگ functions here as an intensifier meaning large/magnificent.
segments.s186-g29: This compact clause uses poetic word order: ورانام ("my/that renown/heroic name") followed by گستهم (an archaic past form meaning "I wandered/ I spread/ I went about/ I vaunted") and then a proper name or epithet گژدهم with خوان after it. خوان here is used in a verbal/performative sense (related to calling or reciting), so the whole line expresses the speaker proclaiming a name or singing a name. The sequence is terse because of epic poetic style.
segments.s186-g30: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause "for/which/whose"; لرزان بود is a predicative adjective plus past auxiliary meaning "was trembling"; پیل is the subject "the elephant"; ازو (poetic contraction of از او) means "from him/because of him"; ز استخوان literally "from the bones" — together: "for the elephant trembled from him to the bones" (i.e. trembled to its very bones because of him). Note the past tense auxiliary بود is the standard past in classical Persian.
segments.s186-g31: پسش again means "behind it/after it." گرگ پیکر is a compound meaning "wolf‑formed / bearing the figure of a wolf" (گرگ + پیکر), and درفشی دراز is "a long banner/standard" (دراز = "long" placed after the noun in poetic word order). The whole phrase describes a long wolf‑shaped standard following behind.
segments.s186-g32: بگردش here functions in context as a spatial phrase meaning "around it / about it" (poetic contraction of به گردِش), not as the imperative listed in the lexicon; بسی is an intensifier meaning "many/very numerous" in this context; مردم رزمساز is a compound noun phrase "people/men who make war" — i.e. "battle‑ready men/warriors." Together: "and around it many warriors."
segments.s186-g41: ورا (vora) is a prepositional/locative clitic here meaning 'to/at it' and heads the phrase 'ورا گرد' = 'around it'. گرد is the postposition/adverb 'around'. شیدوش is used as a noun ('attendant, milker' in the lexicon) placed after the locative phrase; دارد is the present-third-person auxiliary 'has/holds' (here: 'keeps/places') and بپای is a verbal/adverbial infinitival/old-form meaning 'lying in wait / at the foot'. Together the clause literally says 'Around it (vora) around (gard) attendant(s) he has (dârad) at-foot (bepây)', i.e. 'attendants stand about it at its feet.'
segments.s186-g42: چو here means 'as/like' (simile). کوهی is an adjective/epithet 'mountain(-like)' used substantively ('a mountain'). همی is the classical progressive/continuous particle marking ongoing action. اندر آید is a two-word verb phrase: اندر (in/from/within) + آید (present 'comes') — together 'comes forth' or 'advances from'. ز جای is the prepositional phrase 'from its place' (ز = poetic 'from', جای = 'place').
segments.s186-g43: درفش is the subject 'the banner/standard'. گرازست is a predicate formed with the copula -ست (old poetic 'is') attached to 'graz' meaning 'boar-like' — 'the standard is boar-like'. پیکر گراز is an appositive/noun phrase 'the figure/body of a boar' specifying the form of the standard.
segments.s186-g44: سپاهی is the noun 'army/men' and is followed by two adjectival/participial modifiers: کمندافگن ('lasso-casting', an adjectival compound) and رزم‌ساز ('rivaling maker-of-battle' — here 'rendezvous' as someone who makes/engages in battle). و simply links the two modifiers. ساز here is used in the sense of 'maker/means' rather than musical instrument; the construction attributes these warlike qualities to the troop.
segments.s186-g45: درفشی here names the standard/standard-bearer. کجا functions rhetorically as a relative/locative introducer ('where/whose' in epic diction), linking to پیکرش ('its figure/body') which is specified as گاومیش ('water‑buffalo'). The order is typical of classical Persian poetic compression: 'a standard whose body is a buffalo.'
segments.s186-g46: سپاه is 'host/army' followed by positional prepositional phrases: از پس ('from/at the rear' — از + پس = 'from behind') and ز پیش ('from/in front' — ز + پیش = 'from before/in front'). و links the two coordinated groups, so the line describes an army at the rear and spear‑bearers at the front.
segments.s186-g47: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative construction: چنان ('thus/so') + دان (imperative 'consider/know' in classical diction) meaning 'consider it so'. که introduces the content clause that follows. آن شهره فرهاد راست is a nominal phrase 'that famed Farhad is true/real' (شهره used adjectivally 'famed', راست 'true/real').
segments.s186-g48: که starts the explanatory/subordinate clause 'that ...'. گویی is used idiomatically as 'one might say / as if' but the provided lexical senses do not include this verbal use (so index is -1). مگر here functions rhetorically ('perhaps/indeed / rhetorically') and با سپهرست راست forms a prepositional phrase 'with the heavens (سپهر) + -ست (copula) + راست (true)' meaning 'as if he were truly with/under the heavens' (i.e. exalted or heaven‑aligned). The clause as a whole is a simile/epithet reinforcing the truth/fame of Farhad.
segments.s186-g33: "بزیر اندرش زنگه شاوران" is a compact, somewhat archaic phrase meaning 'beneath/under him a ring of advisers.' بزیر is a prepositional/adverbial element placed before the rest (literally 'under'); اندرش is the locative/prepositional اندر plus the enclitic -ش 'his', so 'within/at his (side)'; زنگه here is a poetic noun 'ring/circle' that links directly to شاوران (no ezafe particle is written in epic Persian), so the whole string reads 'a ring of advisers beneath him.'
segments.s186-g34: "دلیران و گردان و کنداوران" is a simple coordination of nouns listing those who made up the ring. دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave ones'; the conjunction و links items; گردان is a collective/noun 'troop/ranks'; کنداوران is another plural noun ('excavators' / sappers) — all are nouns in a series with no copula required in Persian poetry.
segments.s186-g35: "درفشی پرستار پیکر چو ماه" names the person: درفشی 'the standard-bearer' (subject) + پرستار 'guardian/attendant' of پیکر 'figure/body'. چو ماه is a simile placed after پیکر ('a figure like the moon'). No copula is necessary; the phrase strings nominal descriptors together in typical epic diction.
segments.s186-g36: "تنش لعل و جعد از حریر سیاه" describes his appearance/attire. تنش = تن + -ش ('his body'); لعل و جعد are two coordinated nouns ('ruby' [i.e. ruby-colored torso/cheeks] and 'curl(s)' — poetic attributes); از introduces the material/source 'of/from' and حریر سیاه 'black silk' (noun + postpositive adjective). The clause omits an explicit copula, a common poetic device: the nouns function predicatively of تنش.
segments.s186-g37: "ورا بیژن گیو راند همی" places the directional object/phrase before the subjects for emphasis: ورا 'toward him' (directional/accusative), then the two proper names 'بیژن گیو' as coordinated subjects, followed by the past verb راند 'drove/rode (at)'. The enclitic/particle همی after the verb is the archaic/epic iterative marker (habitual/continued action) often used with past verbs in Ferdowsi.
segments.s186-g38: "که خون بآسمان برفشاند همی" is a subordinate/result clause introduced by که 'so that/that'. خون 'blood' is the direct object, بآسمان 'to the sky' is the directional prepositional phrase (ب + آسمان), and برفشاند is the verb 'to spatter/splash upon' (here in the same iterative/poetic aspect with همی). Together the clause explains the effect of their riding.
segments.s186-g39: "درفشی کجا پیکرش هست ببر" is a terse, interrogative/exclamatory line typical in epic verse. درفشی 'the banner-bearer' opens the question; کجا 'where' queries the location; پیکرش 'his body' (پیکر + -ش) is raised as the thing asked about; هست is the copula 'is/does exist'; ببر here is a noun 'tiger' placed immediately after the clause as a dramatic apposition/agent (rather than an imperative). The word order is emphatic/elliptical rather than a full prose sentence.
segments.s186-g40: "همی بشکند زو میان هژبر" is a short predictive clause: همی is the iterative/emphatic particle; بشکند (subjunctive/epic present sense) 'will break/rend' is the verb; ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/of' and و here attaches to form the compound prepositional phrase z-o (from him); میان هژبر = 'in/among/between Hojbar' (هژبر a proper name). Grammatically it reads '(a tiger) will rend (him) from among Hojbar' — an elliptic, image-driven line rather than strict prose syntax.
segments.s186-g49: This is an interrogative noun phrase. درفشی (darfash/i) = 'standard/banner' is the head; کجا (kojâ) here functions as a rhetorical 'which/where (is)' introducing the question. پیکرش = پیکر + -ش is a possessed noun 'its/whose figure', and دیزه گرگ is a noun-noun grouping 'the fortress (dizeh) wolf', i.e. 'the wolf of the fortress' or 'fortress‑wolf' as the depicted creature.
segments.s186-g50: A nominal clause with an omitted copula (typical in epic Persian). نشان is 'emblem/token' and سپهدار گیو is a juxtaposed title + name 'the commander Giv' (no ezafe is written). سترگ is a postpositive quality/noun ('sight, gaze') describing or appositive to نشان; the sense of existence/identity is left implicit rather than expressed by a verb.
segments.s186-g51: Same pattern as the previous line: درفشی 'banner/standard' + کجا 'which/where' introduces the question; شیر پیکر بزر is a noun phrase 'a lion (شیر) — figure (پیکر) great (بزر)', with the adjective بزر following the head noun in poetic order.
segments.s186-g52: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding banner: 'which/that Goudarz Keshvad (گودرز کشواد) دارد (has) بسر'. دارد is the present indicative 'has' and بسر here is a poetic/archaic ordering of بر سر ('upon the head'), so the clause means 'that Goudarz Keshvad bears/has upon his head'. Note the compound personal name گودرز کشواد is presented without an explicit linker.
segments.s186-g53: This clause uses the archaic/copulative suffix -ست attached to a noun: پلنگست = پلنگ + -ست 'is a leopard' (predicate). درفشی is the topic 'the banner', and پیکر گراز = 'body/figure (پیکر) a boar (گراز)' gives a further nominal description. The order is topical and descriptive, typical of Ferdowsi's cataloguing.
segments.s186-g54: پس 'then/so' links the thought; ریونیزست is another archaic formation with -ست meaning 'is raven‑like/blackish' (a predicative adjective). با کام و ناز is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with desire and coquetry/pride' (با = 'with'), where و simply coordinates کام and ناز.
segments.s186-g55: Again the interrogative template: درفشی 'banner' + کجا 'which', آهویش = آهو + -ش 'its gazelle' (possessive suffix) and پیکرست = پیکر + -ست 'is a figure'. The copula is fused (-ست) and the possessive -ش marks that the gazelle is the banner's depicted form.
segments.s186-g56: که introduces the explanatory/relative clause: 'that/because Goudarz, distressed (نستوه), is with (با) an army.' لشکرست is again the predicative formation with -ست ('is/has a host'), so the clause says Goudarz is beset/vexed and is with a host — a compact, slightly elliptical way to link the banner image to the martial situation.
segments.s186-g9: تو (subject pronoun) + گفتی (2nd singular past) is a simple reported-speech clause 'you said'. به introduces a locative/target phrase; کان اندرون is a noun phrase meaning 'the interior of the mine' (کان here = 'mine/ore', اندرون = 'inside'). زر نماند is a verbless clause in effect where نماند (past of ماندن with negative prefix ن-) = 'did not remain / was not left', so the phrase means 'no gold remained'.
segments.s186-g10: برآمد is a 3rd-person past verb 'arose/appeared'. یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / a' placed before the noun (typical Persian word order). ابر is the subject that appears; the conjunction و links a second coordinated clause گوهر فشاند where فشاند is 3rd-person past 'scattered / cast' and گوهر is its object 'gems/jewels'.
segments.s186-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' marking source or cause. بانگ تبیره is a genitival phrase 'the cry/sound of tabireh' (بانگ = 'sound/cry'); میان دو کوه is a locative phrase 'between two mountains' that follows and specifies where the sound was heard.
segments.s186-g12: دل کرگس is a possessive noun–noun sequence 'the heart of the vulture' (literal/figurative). اندر هوا is a locative phrase 'in the air'; شد is the auxiliary/past verb 'became' and pairs with the predicate adjective ستوه 'weary', so شد ستوه = 'became weary'.
segments.s186-g13: چنین گفت is a formulaic order 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', followed by گفت 'said') introducing reported speech. کاکنون ('کا اکنون' fused) is a temporal adverb 'now/at this moment', and درفش مهان is the noun phrase 'the banner of the guests/nobles' (object or topic of the reported utterance).
segments.s186-g14: بگو is a 2nd-person imperative 'say / tell'. و coordinates the next imperative. مدار here functions as a prohibitive/negative directive 'do not keep / do not hide' governing the following phrase ایچ گونه نهان; ایچ گونه is an archaic demonstrative/adverbial 'in this way / thus' (ایچ = 'this/so', گونه = 'manner'), and نهان is the adjective 'hidden', so the sequence means 'and do not keep it hidden in this manner.'
segments.s186-g15: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' marking the indirect object, followed by گفت 'said'). کان introduces reported content ('that / as'), and پیل پیکر is a compound adjective 'elephant-bodied / elephant-shaped' placed before the head noun درفش 'standard/banner', so 'کان پیل پیکر درفش' = 'that the banner was elephant-shaped.'
segments.s186-g16: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing associated elements: سواران 'the riders' and آن تیغهای بنفش 'those purple swords'. آن is a demonstrative 'that/those' pointing to specific items; تیغهای shows plural by the suffix -های; the attributive adjective بنفش follows the noun (typical Persian order).

sentences.s186-s9: Who, amid the host, could be owner of such implements and of this apparatus?
sentences.s186-s10: When the wretch heard his words, he thus gave answer: 'O king,'
sentences.s186-s11: Then behind him stood Tus the spahbed, who bore a grudge and was hostile in combat toward him.
sentences.s186-s12: Another standard-bearer stood behind him; his form was like the shining sun.
sentences.s186-s1: The horsemen reached the mountain's ridge; the host came in, band after band.
sentences.s186-s2: The commander, with thirty thousand lancers — all combatants in the field of battle.
sentences.s186-s3: Cavalry and infantry, great in strength; all sword‑bearing and all spear‑bearing.
sentences.s186-s4: From the abundance of golden shreds and golden standards, and from (even) golden 'Gopal' and gilded shoes.
sentences.s186-s29: What proud mark does the banner bear — a token from Bahram Goudarz of the Keshvadgan?
sentences.s186-s30: They were all lion‑hearts, both foot and horse; I will tell them one by one — the affair is long.
sentences.s186-s31: When each one in turn spoke of his token, that king of kings stepped forward.
sentences.s186-s32: All — nobles and the world alike — looked on, and from joy his steed bloomed like a flower.
sentences.s186-s13: Your father's brother, endowed with farr and desire, is the spahbed Fariborz, called Kavus.
sentences.s186-s14: Behind him was a large banner with a moon‑shaped figure, many brave men and a great cloud of dust.
sentences.s186-s15: I vaunted my name and called out Gozdaham — for the elephant from him trembled to the very bones.

sentences.s186-s24: Consider it thus: that famed Farhad is true — as if one might say he is truly allied with the heavens.

sentences.s186-s25: Which standard has as its figure the fortress-wolf; the emblem of the commander Giv is (so) of wide sight.

sentences.s186-s6: From the cry of the trumpet between two mountains, the vulture's heart in the air became weary.
sentences.s186-s7: Thus he said: 'Now, speak of the banner of the guests, and do not keep it hidden in this way.'
sentences.s186-s8: He said to him that the standard was elephant-shaped — the riders and those purple swords.
